Bowel Actions.title: Bowel Actions per Day
Bowel Actions.content: A healthy baseline varies wildly from person to person. Medically, anywhere from 3 times a day to 3 times a week is considered normal. Tracking sudden deviations from your personal baseline helps identify dietary triggers or stress impacts.
Bowel Actions.actionable_tip: Look for patterns between high-frequency days and your fiber or caffeine intake.

Bad.title: Low Mood
Bad.content: A sudden, unexplained drop in mood can be linked directly to gut inflammation. Over 90% of your body's serotonin (the 'happy chemical') is produced in your digestive tract. Disruptions here heavily impact your emotional state.
Bad.actionable_tip: Check your logs for highly processed or inflammatory foods eaten 12-24 hours prior. If low mood persists, please reach out for support.

Okay.title: Neutral Mood
Okay.content: Your gut-brain axis is functioning normally, though you may be experiencing mild systemic stress or fatigue preventing a fully optimal state.
Okay.actionable_tip: Focus on hydration and whole foods today to give your microbiome a rest.

Good.title: Optimal Mood
Good.content: Excellent gut-brain connection! A happy microbiome produces plenty of neurotransmitters, leading to stable energy and a positive mental state.
Good.actionable_tip: Review what you ate yesterday—you may have found a highly compatible combination of foods.

Nausea.title: Nausea
Nausea.content: Often a sign of delayed gastric emptying or a localized reaction to a trigger food. If nausea consistently follows meals, it may be linked to high-fat foods, strong spices, or specific intolerances.
Nausea.actionable_tip: Note if symptoms occur immediately after eating (stomach) or several hours later (small intestine).

Vomit.title: Vomiting
Vomit.content: A severe acute reflex indicating your body is forcefully expelling an irritant. This is usually caused by foodborne illness, a strong intolerance, or viral infection.
Vomit.actionable_tip: Focus strictly on hydration and electrolyte replacement. Avoid solid foods until the reflex passes.

Heartburn.title: Heartburn
Heartburn.content: Occurs when stomach acid flows back into the esophagus. Common dietary triggers include acidic foods, spicy dishes, caffeine, and high-fat meals which relax the esophageal sphincter.
Heartburn.actionable_tip: Avoid lying down for at least 2 hours after a triggering meal.

Cramps.title: Intestinal Cramps
Cramps.content: Spasms in the intestinal wall, often caused by trapped gas, acute localized inflammation, or an overly rapid transit time as your gut tries to move an irritant.
Cramps.actionable_tip: Gentle movement, hydration, or a warm compress can help relax intestinal muscles.

Bloating.title: Bloating
Bloating.content: Usually caused by excess gas production when gut bacteria ferment undigested carbohydrates (like FODMAPs) in the large intestine. It can also be related to delayed transit time.
Bloating.actionable_tip: If bloating is chronic, consider tracking high-FODMAP foods like onions, garlic, and wheat.

Gas.title: Excess Gas
Gas.content: A natural byproduct of gut bacteria fermenting carbohydrates. While normal in moderate amounts, excessive or painful gas usually points to specific unabsorbed sugars or fibers.
Gas.actionable_tip: Track your intake of legumes, artificial sweeteners, and cruciferous vegetables.

Constipation.title: Constipation
Constipation.content: A slowed transit time causes the colon to absorb too much water, making stools hard to pass. This is frequently linked to low dietary fiber, dehydration, or changes in routine.
Constipation.actionable_tip: Increase water intake alongside soluble fiber sources like oats or psyllium husk.

Normal.title: Normal Digestion
Normal.content: Perfect digestion! Your transit time and water absorption are perfectly balanced, indicating your current diet and stress levels are well-tolerated.
Normal.actionable_tip: Keep doing what you're doing. Save recent meals as safe baselines.

Diarrhoea.title: Diarrhoea
Diarrhoea.content: A rapid transit time where the colon doesn't absorb enough water. Your body is likely trying to rapidly flush out an irritant, pathogen, or unabsorbed osmotic sugars.
Diarrhoea.actionable_tip: Stay hydrated and temporarily avoid high-fat, high-sugar, or heavily spiced foods.

Headache.title: Headache
Headache.content: Can be caused directly by dehydration resulting from GI distress, or by systemic inflammation originating in the gut crossing the blood-brain barrier.
Headache.actionable_tip: Drink a large glass of water and check for known trigger foods (like aged cheeses or artificial additives) in the last 24 hours.

Fatigue.title: Fatigue
Fatigue.content: Digestive distress takes a massive amount of cellular energy. If your body is fighting a food intolerance or managing gut inflammation, it will divert energy away from your muscles and brain.
Fatigue.actionable_tip: Ensure you are getting enough hydration, as fluid loss from GI distress compounds physical fatigue.

Brainfog.title: Brain Fog
Brainfog.content: Systemic inflammation originating in the gut can interfere with neural function, leading to difficulty focusing, poor memory, and a feeling of mental cloudiness.
Brainfog.actionable_tip: Track this alongside systemic symptoms like fatigue to spot hidden inflammatory triggers.

Acne.title: Acne & Breakouts
Acne.content: The gut-skin axis is heavily researched. Imbalances in gut bacteria or spikes in blood sugar can increase sebum production and drive systemic skin inflammation.
Acne.actionable_tip: Look for patterns involving high-sugar, high-dairy, or highly processed food intake.

Rash.title: Skin Rashes
Rash.content: Sudden rashes, eczema flare-ups, or hives can indicate a histamine reaction, a localized food allergy, or a compromised gut lining (leaky gut).
Rash.actionable_tip: Note new foods or skin products. If accompanied by swelling or difficulty breathing, seek medical attention immediately.

Unicorn.title: Unicorn Status
Unicorn.content: You've discovered the mythical baseline! Your digestion is flawless, your energy is boundless, and you probably sweat glitter. Whatever you ate today, keep doing it.
Unicorn.actionable_tip: Save today's meal log as a 'Safe Day' template.
